front: Opera: title libertine is dragged to hell by a statue of the Commendatore when he refuses to repent
Opera: full title is Il dissoluto punito (The Rake Punished); libretto by Lorenzo Da Ponte; premiered in Prague in 1787
Opera: Pauline Viardot bought the original score and built a shrine to it; Tchaikovsky wrote his Orchestral Suite No. 4 (Mozartiana) after seeing the score at her home
Opera: Kierkegaard's essay 'The Immediate Stages of the Erotic' (in Either/Or) describes the title character as 'not character but essentially life' and 'absolutely musical'
Opera: Gustav Mahler cut its final sextet ('this is how all evildoers end') when he conducted it; Alfred Roller designed a white urn-embroidered jacket for a production
Opera: Leporello sings the Catalogue Aria ('Madamina, il catalogo è questo') to Donna Elvira, claiming 1,003 Spanish women among his master's conquests
work: The Catalogue Aria ("Madamina, il catalogo è questo")

front: Opera: Catalogue Aria interpolates dotted arpeggiated figures in D major between repetitions of 'mille e tre' (one thousand and three)
work: The Catalogue Aria ("Madamina, il catalogo è questo")

front: Opera: the duet 'La ci darem la mano' between the title character and Zerlina (a peasant girl) prompted Schumann to exclaim 'Hats off, gentlemen — a genius!' after Chopin wrote variations on it
work: "La ci darem la mano" (Duet – Don Giovanni and Zerlina)

front: Opera: 'La ci darem la mano' shifts abruptly from 2/4 to 6/8 time when the two characters sing 'Andiam!' together
work: "La ci darem la mano" (Duet – Don Giovanni and Zerlina)

front: Opera: the Commendatore's three knocks are accompanied by loud D minor chords from the overture; he chants 'Pentiti!' while the title character refuses, shouting 'No!' on a high D
Opera: Leporello hides under the table in the final scene and was too frightened to invite the statue to dinner himself; the statue answers the invitation with an affirmative 'Si'
Opera: overture begins in D minor with a loud tutti chord followed by crashing syncopated chords in the violins and violas; imitates the opening of Gluck's Alceste

Opera: in the overture, flutes and violins play an ascending and descending D minor scale, then eerily ascending major and melodic minor scales; D minor chords are reharmonized into diminished sevenths for the final scene
Opera: Act I masked ball features three simultaneous dances — a minuet in 3/4, a contradance in 2/4, and a waltz in 3/8 — played by the pit orchestra and two onstage ensembles
work: The Ball Scene / Three Simultaneous Dances

front: Opera: Wye Jamison Allanbrook's analysis of dance rhythms and social class influenced Peter Sellars's 1988 Trump Tower production
work: The Ball Scene / Three Simultaneous Dances

front: Opera: onstage band at the feast scene plays 'Non più andrai' from the composer's own Marriage of Figaro; the title character says 'I know this only too well!'
work: The Feast Scene / Quotes from Other Operas

front: Opera: feast scene onstage band also quotes Una cosa rara (Martín y Soler) and Fra i due litiganti (Sarti)
work: The Feast Scene / Quotes from Other Operas

front: Opera: Zerlina mollifies her jealous fiancé Masetto by inviting him to beat her in the aria 'Batti, batti, o bel Masetto'
Opera: title character serenades Donna Elvira's maid while accompanying himself on the mandolin in 'Deh vieni alla finestra'
Opera: Leporello opens the opera with 'Notte e giorno faticar' (Night and day I toil), sung to quarter notes alternating between F and C
